parentQuestion: Which movement of ions produces EPSPs in cochlea hair cells?

scaffolds.q: EPSPs (excitatory postsynaptic potentials) are depolarizations that excite neurons.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! EPSPs make neurons more likely to fire — they're excitatory signals.

scaffolds.q: Depolarization typically occurs when POSITIVE ions flow INTO a cell.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Adding positive charges makes the inside less negative (depolarization).

scaffolds.q: In most neurons, Na+ influx or K+ efflux changes membrane potential.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Na+ entry depolarizes; K+ exit hyperpolarizes in typical neurons.

scaffolds.q: Hair cells in the cochlea are bathed in endolymph at their top (apical) surface.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The scala media contains endolymph surrounding hair cell stereocilia.

scaffolds.q: Endolymph is unusual: HIGH in K+ (~150mM) AND positively charged (+80mV).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The endocochlear potential is +80mV — very unusual for extracellular fluid.

scaffolds.q: Hair cell interior is about -70mV — normal for cells.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! So the gradient from endolymph (+80mV) to cell interior (-70mV) = 150mV driving force!

scaffolds.q: Because of this huge electrical gradient, K+ flows INTO cochlear hair cells (unusual!).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Normally K+ exits cells. Here the +80mV endolymph drives K+ inward.

scaffolds.q: Ca²⁺ also enters through mechanotransduction channels, triggering neurotransmitter release.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Ca²⁺ influx is essential for synaptic vesicle fusion.

scaffolds.q: K⁺ and Ca²⁺ INFLUX depolarizes cochlear hair cells — the answer is C.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Due to the unique +80mV endocochlear potential, K⁺ enters (not exits) along with Ca²⁺.
